Never cite from memory — only from a local `references/<file>.md` you opened and read in this session.
186	- **Document every change** in your report, including which `references/<file>.md` files you opened (under "References consulted").

188	### What you MUST NOT do
189	- **Do NOT reference project history.** No "since iteration N," "after our failed attempt," or session narrative. The blueprint must read as a standalone mathematical document.
190	- **Do NOT include Lean implementation details.** No tactic suggestions, typeclass wiring notes, or Lean code structure hints in the prose. If a Lean note is needed, it belongs in `.lean` files (the plan agent will use `lean-scaffolder` for that).
191	- **Do NOT add `\leanok` markers.** `\leanok` is earned by a sorry-free Lean proof and set by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase — never by you. (`\mathlibok` is different: you MAY add it, but ONLY on a Mathlib dependency anchor — see "Mathlib dependency anchors" below — never on the project's own to-be-proved declarations.)
192	- **Do NOT edit other chapters.** Even when you spot a related issue, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" instead of fixing it.
193	- **Do NOT edit `content.tex`** (the top-level blueprint file that `\input`s the chapters).
194	- **Do NOT edit `.lean` files** or any other state file.
195	- **Do NOT write Lean syntax** — keep the chapter mathematical, not syntactic.
196	- **Do NOT write literal `REF` placeholders.** Never produce prose like "Definition~REF" or "Sections REF–REF". Cross-reference declarations with `\cref{<label>}` using the real label (look it up in the chapter or via `leandag`); when the target genuinely has no label yet, describe it by name in prose. The blueprint-doctor flags every literal `REF` as a malformed reference.
197	- **Do NOT interleave math delimiters.** Pick `\( … \)` for a formula and close it with `\)` — never produce `$ … \( … \) … $` or `X\(, hence \)Y` inversions; the doctor's `math-delim` lint flags every such site and the rendered output shreds mid-formula.
198	- **Do NOT paste bare label ids into prose.** "Thm.~th:main" is not a reference — write `\cref{...}` for project declarations, or the source's human-readable number ("Thm.~4.8") when quoting external material.
199	- **Do NOT use undefined macros.** Every `\foo` your prose needs must exist in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex` or be `\providecommand`'d at the top of your chapter (chapter-local is fine; report a "needs macro" note for shared ones).
200	- **Do NOT touch protected blocks.** Your invocation prompt's "Protected by the mathematician" section lists `archon-protected.yaml` rules. A `statement`-protected label's declaration block (statement + `\label`/`\lean`/`\uses`) is frozen — you may still write its `\begin{proof}`; an `all`-protected label is entirely off-limits. You may freely `\uses{}` protected labels from other blocks. If your directive conflicts with a protection rule, stop and report the conflict instead of editing.
201	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
202	- **Do NOT fabricate citations.** Never write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from memory. If you don't have a local `references/<file>.md` containing the source text, dispatch a retriever and wait. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical is the discipline check — if you cannot truthfully point to the file you read, the citation block is not allowed in the chapter.
203	- **Do NOT translate or restate the verbatim quote.** `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` contain the source's original language, original notation, every word as-is. Project-notation rewrites belong in the rendered prose body, not in the verbatim comment.
204	
205	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
206	
207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
208	
209	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
210	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
211	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
212	
213	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
214	
215	```latex
216	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
218	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
222	\end{lemma}
223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
234	
235	## Reading references
236	
237	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
238	
239	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
240	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
241	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
242	
243	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.

245	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
246	
247	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
248	
249	Conditions for dispatch:
250	
251	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
252	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
253	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
254	
255	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
256	
257	```
258	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
259	  --name reference-retriever \
260	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
261	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
262	  --write-domain 'references/**'
263	```
264	
265	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
266	
267	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.

269	## Workflow
270	
271	1. Read your directive completely.
272	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
273	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
274	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow. For each block that derives from an external source, identify the specific local `references/<file>.md` containing the verbatim statement (and proof, if applicable). If no local file covers the statement, list it as "retrieval needed" for the next step.
275	5. **For every "retrieval needed" item, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and **wait** for it to return. THEN open and read the newly-written `references/<slug>.md` before drafting the citing block. Do not draft citation blocks against pending or imagined sources.
276	6. Make the edits. For each citation block: copy `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` content character-by-character from the local reference file — original language, original notation, every word. Do not transcribe from a window of the file you "remember reading"; have the file open and copy.
277	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`). Spot-check that every `% SOURCE:` line has a non-empty `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical and that the named file exists.
278	8. **Verify dependencies with `leandag`** (see the section below): confirm your chapter introduced no broken `\uses{}` and left none of your blocks unintentionally isolated. Fix the edges and re-check.
279	9. Write your report. List under "References consulted" every local file you opened in step 2 and step 5.
280	
281	## Verifying dependencies with leandag
282	
283	`leandag` parses the real Lean ↔ blueprint dependency DAG, so use it to check your `\uses{}` instead of trusting your memory of the graph. It is read-only on the blueprint (it never edits your chapter); run it after you make edits:
284	
285	```
286	leandag build --json                       # report: unknown_uses, unmatched_lean, isolated count
287	leandag query --isolated --chapter <c>     # isolated nodes in your chapter (no edges in or out)
288	leandag show isolated                       # all isolated nodes, project-wide
289	```
290	
291	`leandag` is on the same PATH as `archon`; it is the one tool you use to query the DAG.
292	
293	What to do with the output, scoped to **your chapter**:
294	
295	- **`unknown_uses` naming a label in your chapter** → you wrote a `\uses{}` pointing at a label that doesn't exist. Fix the label, or — if the target genuinely belongs in your chapter and the directive calls for it — add the missing declaration. If the missing label lives in another chapter, leave a correct `\uses{}` to it and note it under "Notes for Plan Agent" (you don't edit that chapter).
296	- **A block you wrote shows up as isolated** → it has no `\uses{}` out and nothing uses it. This is almost always a *missing edge*: add the `\uses{}` that ties it to the declarations its statement/proof actually depends on (and check whether a sibling result should `\uses{}` it). Only treat an isolated block as removable when your directive explicitly authorized removing it.
297	- **Removal** (directive-authorized only): delete the orphaned block cleanly and record it in your report. Never remove the goal or a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
298	
299	Keep `leandag` edits inside your assigned chapter — fixing a dependency that lives in another chapter is a "Notes for Plan Agent" item, not a cross-chapter edit.

91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

2	\label{chap:Cohomology_AcyclicResolution}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/AcyclicResolution.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, \S Higher derived functors
5	% (Definition \texttt{definition-derived-functor} (Tag 0157), Lemmas
6	% \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic} (Tag 015C), \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D),
7	% \texttt{lemma-leray-acyclicity} (Tag 015E) and Proposition

10	
11	\section{Setup and motivation}
12	
13	This chapter isolates the one abstract, scheme-independent piece of homological
14	algebra on which the {\v C}ech computation of the higher direct images rests: an
15	\emph{acyclic resolution computes the right-derived functor}. The companion chapter
16	\ref{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage} builds, from a separated quasi-compact
17	morphism \(f : X \to S\) and an affine open cover, an explicit complex of
18	\(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules --- the relative {\v C}ech complex
19	\(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) --- whose terms are
20	pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the affine intersections. To identify the
21	cohomology of that complex with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\), one observes that the
22	{\v C}ech complex is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) by objects on which the higher
23	direct images vanish (the affine acyclicity of
24	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}), and then invokes the present theorem with
25	\(G = f_*\). This single homological lemma thereby replaces \emph{both} the
26	{\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequence and the Leray spectral sequence.
27	
28	Throughout, \(\mathcal{A}\) and \(\mathcal{B}\) are abelian categories,
29	\(\mathcal{A}\) has enough injectives (so that injective resolutions exist and the
30	right-derived functors below are everywhere defined), and
31	\(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) is an additive functor. For \(n \in \mathbb{N}\)
32	we write \(R^n G\) for the \(n\)-th right-derived functor of \(G\)
33	(a functor \(\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\)), constructed from injective resolutions:
34	for an injective resolution \(X \to I^\bullet\) one has
35	\((R^n G)(X) \cong H^n\bigl(G(I^\bullet)\bigr)\)
36	(Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} below). When in addition \(G\) is
37	left exact --- equivalently, \(G\) preserves finite limits, which holds for any \(G\)
38	that is a right adjoint, such as a pushforward \(f_*\) --- one has \(R^0 G \cong G\)
39	(Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}).
40	
41	\section{Known results}
42	
43	The following three well-known results underpin the injective-resolution
44	construction of \(R^n G\) and are the foundational inputs to the argument.
45	
46	\begin{lemma}[Right-derived functor via an injective resolution]
47	  \label{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
48	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.isoRightDerivedObj}
49	  \mathlibok
50	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
51	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives,
52	  \(\mathcal{B}\) abelian, and \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) additive. For an
53	  object \(X\) of \(\mathcal{A}\), a choice of injective resolution
54	  \(I\) of \(X\) (an exact complex \(0 \to X \to I^0 \to I^1 \to \cdots\) with each
55	  \(I^n\) injective), and every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), there is a canonical
56	  isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)
57	  \[
58	    (R^n G)(X)
59	      \;\cong\;
60	      H^n\bigl(G(I^\bullet)\bigr),
61	  \]
62	  where \(G(I^\bullet)\) is the cochain complex obtained by applying \(G\)
63	  degreewise to the resolution complex \(I^\bullet\) and \(H^n\) is its \(n\)-th
64	  cohomology object. This is the defining computation of the right-derived functor
65	  and the seed from which the acyclic-resolution generalization is bootstrapped.
66	\end{lemma}
67	
68	\begin{lemma}[Higher derived functors vanish on injectives]
69	  \label{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}
70	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ}
71	  \mathlibok
72	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
73	  With \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, G\) as above, if \(J\) is an injective object of
74	  \(\mathcal{A}\) then \((R^{n+1} G)(J) = 0\) for every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\); that
75	  is, the higher right-derived functors vanish on injective objects. In particular
76	  every injective object is right-\(G\)-acyclic in the sense of
77	  Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}.
78	\end{lemma}
79	
80	\begin{lemma}[The zeroth derived functor of a left-exact functor]
81	  \label{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
82	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedZeroIsoSelf}
83	  \mathlibok
84	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
85	  With \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, G\) as above, if \(G\) is left exact (preserves
86	  finite limits) then the natural transformation \(G \to R^0 G\) is an isomorphism,
87	  \[
88	    R^0 G \;\cong\; G.
89	  \]
90	\end{lemma}

92	\begin{lemma}[Long exact homology sequence of a short exact sequence of complexes]
93	  \label{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}

95	    CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₂,
96	    CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₃,
97	    CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.δ}
98	  \mathlibok

101	  \[
102	    0 \longrightarrow S.X_1 \longrightarrow S.X_2 \longrightarrow S.X_3
103	      \longrightarrow 0
104	  \]
105	  be a short exact sequence of cochain complexes in \(\mathcal{C}\) --- that is, a
106	  short complex \(S = (S.X_1 \to S.X_2 \to S.X_3)\) of objects of the category of
107	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-indexed cochain complexes \(\mathrm{HomologicalComplex}\,\mathcal{C}\)

166	  derived functor \(RG\)); for an additive --- in our applications, left exact ---
167	  functor \(G\) that intrinsic condition is equivalent to the vanishing of all
168	  \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\), which is the form we adopt here and which is

179	\section{Lifting a short exact sequence to injective resolutions}
180	
181	The passage from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
182	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) to a long exact sequence of
183	right-derived functors requires one geometric input: a short exact sequence of
184	objects must be lifted to a short exact sequence of their injective resolutions
185	which is, moreover, \emph{degreewise split}. This is the dual Horseshoe Lemma. It is the single genuinely new homological-algebra construction the argument
186	requires; individual injective resolutions (provided by enough injectives) furnish
187	the building blocks, and the horseshoe construction assembles them into the required
188	lift.
189	
190	\subsection{The horseshoe construction, step by step}
191	
192	The construction proceeds by induction on degree; we isolate its independent moving
193	parts as the sub-lemmas below, from which the horseshoe lemma is assembled. Throughout,
194	\(I_A\) and \(I_C\) are fixed injective resolutions of \(A\) and \(C\) (they exist
195	because \(\mathcal{A}\) has enough injectives), and we write
196	\(d_A, d_C\) for their differentials and \(I_A^n, I_C^n\) for their degree-\(n\) terms.
197	The middle resolution is built degreewise as the biproduct
198	\(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\). All seven statements are structural pieces of the dual
199	Horseshoe Lemma (dual of Weibel, \emph{An Introduction to Homological Algebra}, Lemma
200	2.2.8); no new source is introduced.
201	
202	\begin{lemma}[Degree term: a finite biproduct of injectives is injective]
203	  \label{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
204	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Injective.instBiprod}
205	  \mathlibok
206	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
207	  In an abelian category, if \(P\) and \(Q\) are injective objects then their biproduct
208	  \(P \oplus Q\) is injective. Consequently each degree term
209	  \(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\) of the middle complex is injective, being the
210	  biproduct of the injective terms \(I_A^n\) and \(I_C^n\) of the two chosen
211	  resolutions.
212	\end{lemma}
213	
214	\begin{lemma}[Degree term: the biproduct short complex is split exact]
215	  \label{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}
216	  \lean{CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.Splitting.ofHasBinaryBiproduct}
217	  \mathlibok
218	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
219	  For objects \(P, Q\) of an abelian category the short complex
220	  \[
221	    P \xrightarrow{\ \iota_P\ } P \oplus Q \xrightarrow{\ \pi_Q\ } Q
222	  \]
223	  formed from the biproduct coprojection \(\iota_P\) and projection \(\pi_Q\) carries a
224	  canonical \texttt{ShortComplex.Splitting}; in particular it is a split short exact
225	  sequence. Applied in each degree \(n\) with \(P = I_A^n\), \(Q = I_C^n\), this exhibits
226	  every degree
227	  \[
228	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^n \longrightarrow I_B^n \longrightarrow I_C^n \longrightarrow 0
229	  \]
230	  as split, furnishing the degreewise-splitting data required for the construction.
231	\end{lemma}
232	
233	\begin{lemma}\leanok
234	[Per-stage monomorphism into the biproduct]
235	  \label{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}
236	  \lean{CategoryTheory.mono_biprod_lift_factorThru_of_exact}
237	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
238	  Let \(0 \to S.X_1 \xrightarrow{S.f} S.X_2 \xrightarrow{S.g} S.X_3 \to 0\) be an exact
239	  short complex with \(S.f\) a monomorphism, and let \(\alpha : S.X_1 \to P\),
240	  \(\gamma : S.X_3 \to Q\) be monomorphisms with \(P\) injective. Then the map
241	  \[
242	    \bigl\langle\, \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f)\,,\; S.g \circ \gamma \,\bigr\rangle
243	      \;:\; S.X_2 \longrightarrow P \oplus Q,
244	  \]
245	  whose first component is the injective extension of \(\alpha\) along the mono \(S.f\)
246	  and whose second component is \(S.X_2 \twoheadrightarrow S.X_3 \hookrightarrow Q\), is a
247	  monomorphism. This is the kernel/cokernel step driving each stage of the recursion:
248	  applied to the \(n\)-th cosyzygy short exact sequence with \(P = I_A^{n+1}\),
249	  \(Q = I_C^{n+1}\) it produces the monomorphism of the next cosyzygy into the next
250	  biproduct term, whose cokernel feeds the following stage (and, at the base stage with
251	  the original sequence \(0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0\), the augmentation
252	  \(\beta : B \hookrightarrow I_B^0\)).
253	\end{lemma}
254	
255	\begin{proof}
256	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
257	  \leanok
258	  Write \(u := \langle \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f), S.g \circ \gamma\rangle\). To see
259	  \(u\) is mono it suffices, in an abelian category, to show \(x \circ u = 0 \implies x = 0\)
260	  for any \(x\). Composing \(x \circ u\) with the two biproduct projections gives
261	  \(x \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f) = 0\) and \(x \circ S.g \circ \gamma = 0\);
262	  since \(\gamma\) is mono the latter yields \(x \circ S.g = 0\). Exactness of \(S\) at
263	  \(S.X_2\) (with \(S.f\) mono) lets us lift \(x\) through \(S.f\): there is \(y\) with
264	  \(y \circ S.f = x\). Then \(y \circ \alpha = y \circ S.f \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f)
265	  = x \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f) = 0\), using the defining property
266	  \(S.f \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f) = \alpha\); as \(\alpha\) is mono, \(y = 0\),
267	  hence \(x = y \circ S.f = 0\).
268	\end{proof}

270	\begin{lemma}[Off-diagonal twist and augmentation lifts]
271	  \label{lem:horseshoe_twist}

281	  \[
282	    \tau^n : I_C^n \longrightarrow I_A^{n+1} \qquad (n \in \mathbb{N})

296	\begin{proof}
297	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}
298	  The lifts are constructed by recursion on \(n\). For the augmentation, the first
299	  component of \(\beta\) extends the composite \(A \to I_A^0\) along the monomorphism
300	  \(A \hookrightarrow B\) using injectivity of \(I_A^0\); the second component is
301	  \(B \twoheadrightarrow C \to I_C^0\).
302	  At the inductive step we have \(\tau^{n}\) (with \(\tau^{n} : I_C^{n} \to I_A^{n+1}\))
303	  and seek \(\tau^{n+1} : I_C^{n+1} \to I_A^{n+2}\) with
304	  \(\tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n = -\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n\). The right-hand side
305	  \(-\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n : I_C^n \to I_A^{n+2}\) annihilates
306	  \(\ker d_C^n = \operatorname{im} d_C^{n-1}\) --- the equality \(\ker d_C^n =
307	  \operatorname{im} d_C^{n-1}\) being the exactness of the injective resolution \(I_C\) in
308	  the positive degree \(n\), which is part of the defining data of an injective resolution.
309	  Indeed, precomposing with \(d_C^{n-1}\) and using the
310	  previous-stage identity \(\tau^{n} \circ d_C^{n-1} = -\,d_A^{n} \circ \tau^{n-1}\) gives
311	  \(-\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n \circ d_C^{n-1} = d_A^{n+1} \circ d_A^{n} \circ \tau^{n-1} = 0\),
312	  where the final equality is the complex identity \(d_A^{n+1} \circ d_A^{n} = 0\) for the
313	  cochain complex \(I_A\). Hence
314	  \(-\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n\) factors through the image of \(d_C^n\), i.e.\ through the
315	  monomorphism \(\operatorname{im} d_C^n \hookrightarrow I_C^{n+1}\); injectivity of the
316	  target \(I_A^{n+2}\) extends that factorization along this monomorphism to a map
317	  \(\tau^{n+1} : I_C^{n+1} \to I_A^{n+2}\) defined on all of \(I_C^{n+1}\) and satisfying
318	  \(\tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n = -\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n\) by construction. This is exactly
319	  the dual of the inductive lift in the Horseshoe Lemma.
320	\end{proof}

486	\section{The dimension-shift step}
487	
488	The induction driving the comparison theorem rests on a single short-exact-sequence
489	calculation: cutting a resolution into short exact sequences and reading off the
490	long exact sequence of right-derived functors. The latter is not available off the
491	shelf at the level of the derived-functor objects \((R^k G)\); we build it from the
492	complex-level homology long exact sequence
493	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) via the horseshoe lift
494	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}). We isolate the result as the kernel
495	sub-lemma below.

546	% The Stacks proof of Tag 015D derives the long exact sequence from the
547	% delta-functor structure of $RF$ on the derived category $D^{+}(\mathcal{A})$.
548	% The proof below constructs the same sequence at the level of right-derived-functor
549	% objects from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
550	% (Lemma \ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) after a horseshoe lift
551	% (Lemma \ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}); accordingly no verbatim proof
552	% quote from Tag 015D is attached.
553	\begin{proof}
554	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
555	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
556	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}

558	  We first construct the long exact sequence of right-derived functors associated
559	  to \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\). In the classical treatment this sequence is
560	  supplied by the \(\delta\)-functor structure of \(\{R^k G\}\) on the derived
561	  category; here it is built directly from the complex-level homology long exact
562	  sequence via a horseshoe lift.
563	
564	  \emph{Building the long exact sequence.} By the horseshoe lift
565	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) choose injective resolutions
566	  \(I_A, I_J, I_Z\) of \(A, J, Z\) fitting into a short exact sequence of cochain
567	  complexes
568	  \[
569	    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_J \longrightarrow I_Z \longrightarrow 0
570	  \]
571	  that is degreewise split. Apply \(G\) degreewise. Because each degree
572	  \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_J^n \to I_Z^n \to 0\) is a \emph{split} short exact sequence
573	  (so \(I_J^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_Z^n\) compatibly with the maps), the additive
574	  functor \(G\) carries it to a split short exact sequence
575	  \(0 \to G(I_A^n) \to G(I_J^n) \to G(I_Z^n) \to 0\) --- this is where degreewise
576	  splitness is essential, since \(G\) is not assumed exact and would not in general
577	  preserve a non-split short exact sequence. Hence
578	  \[
579	    0 \longrightarrow G(I_A) \longrightarrow G(I_J) \longrightarrow G(I_Z)
580	      \longrightarrow 0
581	  \]
582	  is a short exact sequence of cochain complexes in \(\mathcal{B}\). The
583	  complex-level homology long exact sequence
584	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) applied to it reads
585	  \[
586	    \cdots \to H^k\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to H^k\bigl(G(I_Z)\bigr)
587	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_A)\bigr)
588	      \to H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to \cdots .
589	  \]
590	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} the homology of each applied
591	  resolution computes the right-derived functor:
592	  \(H^k(G(I_A)) \cong (R^k G)(A)\), \(H^k(G(I_J)) \cong (R^k G)(J)\) and
593	  \(H^k(G(I_Z)) \cong (R^k G)(Z)\), naturally in the maps of the sequence. Transporting
594	  the homology long exact sequence across these isomorphisms gives the long exact
595	  sequence of right-derived functors
596	  \[
597	    \cdots \to (R^k G)(J) \to (R^k G)(Z)
598	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to (R^{k+1} G)(J) \to \cdots,
599	  \]
600	  and, using \(R^0 G \cong G\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) at the
601	  bottom, its low-degree initial segment
602	  \[
603	    0 \to G(A) \to G(J) \to G(Z)
604	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to (R^1 G)(J) \to \cdots .
605	  \]
606	
607	  \emph{Killing the acyclic terms.} Because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic
608	  (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}), every term \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\)
609	  vanishes. For \(k \geq 1\) the segment around \((R^k G)(J)\),
610	  \[
611	    \underbrace{(R^{k} G)(J)}_{=0} \to (R^{k} G)(Z)
612	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to \underbrace{(R^{k+1} G)(J)}_{=0},
613	  \]
614	  has both outer terms zero, so the connecting map
615	  \(\delta^k : (R^{k} G)(Z) \to (R^{k+1} G)(A)\) is an isomorphism, which is the
616	  asserted dimension shift. (The lowest-degree segment of the same long exact
617	  sequence yields the cokernel description of \((R^1 G)(A)\); that reading is
618	  recorded separately in Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}.)
619	\end{proof}

662	  \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\), in the proof of
663	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}; here we use only its initial terms, so we
664	  recall the construction briefly and then specialize. By the horseshoe lift
665	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) choose injective resolutions
666	  \(I_A, I_J, I_Z\) of \(A, J, Z\) fitting into a degreewise-split short exact
667	  sequence of cochain complexes \(0 \to I_A \to I_J \to I_Z \to 0\). Applying the
668	  additive functor \(G\) degreewise preserves each split degree, giving a short exact
669	  sequence of complexes \(0 \to G(I_A) \to G(I_J) \to G(I_Z) \to 0\) in
670	  \(\mathcal{B}\); its complex-level homology long exact sequence
671	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}), transported across the
672	  injective-resolution computation \(H^k(G(I_\bullet)) \cong (R^k G)(-)\)
673	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}) and using
674	  \(R^0 G \cong G\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}), has low-degree
675	  initial segment
676	  \[
677	    G(J) \to G(Z) \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to (R^1 G)(J).
678	  \]
679	  Because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}), the right
680	  end \((R^1 G)(J)\) vanishes. Hence the segment
681	  \(G(J) \to G(Z) \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to 0\) is exact with vanishing
682	  right end, so \(\delta^0\) is an epimorphism whose kernel is the image of
683	  \(G(J) \to G(Z)\). Therefore \(\delta^0\) factors through an isomorphism
684	  \(\operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J) \to G(Z)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\ \cong\ } (R^1 G)(A)\),
685	  which is the assertion.
686	\end{proof}

688	\section{Cosyzygies of an acyclic resolution}
689	
690	The comparison theorem decomposes an acyclic resolution into the short exact
691	sequences cut out by its cosyzygies, and reads the cohomology of the applied complex
692	off those sequences using left-exactness of \(G\). We isolate the two ingredients
693	here.
694	
695	\begin{lemma}[Cosyzygy short exact sequences of an acyclic resolution]
696	  \label{lem:cosyzygy_ses}
697	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.cosyzygyShortExact}
698	  \uses{def:right_acyclic}
699	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Proposition
700	  % \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA)
701	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5785--5838).
702	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Say $I^n = 0$ for $n < n_0$. Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
703	  % $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
704	  % $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
705	  % for $n \geq n_0$."
706	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 05TA
707	  (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}).}
708	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be additive and let
709	  \[
710	    0 \longrightarrow A
711	      \longrightarrow J^0 \longrightarrow J^1 \longrightarrow J^2
712	      \longrightarrow \cdots
713	  \]
714	  be an exact resolution of \(A\) with every \(J^n\) right-\(G\)-acyclic. Define the
715	  cosyzygies \(Z^0 := A\) and, for \(m \geq 1\),
716	  \[
717	    Z^m \;:=\; \ker\bigl(J^m \to J^{m+1}\bigr)
718	      \;=\; \operatorname{im}\bigl(J^{m-1} \to J^m\bigr).
719	  \]
720	  Then for every \(m \geq 0\) the sequence
721	  \[
722	    0 \longrightarrow Z^m \longrightarrow J^m \longrightarrow Z^{m+1}
723	      \longrightarrow 0 \tag{$S_m$}
724	  \]
725	  is short exact, with the inclusion \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) the canonical one,
726	  the surjection \(J^m \twoheadrightarrow Z^{m+1}\) the corestriction of
727	  \(J^m \to J^{m+1}\) onto its image, and the middle term \(J^m\) right-\(G\)-acyclic.
728	  In particular \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\).
729	\end{lemma}
730	
731	\begin{proof}
732	  \uses{def:right_acyclic}
733	  Fix \(m \geq 0\). Mono of \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) is immediate: \(Z^m\) is by
734	  definition a subobject of \(J^m\) (for \(m = 0\) via the injection \(A \hookrightarrow
735	  J^0\) of the resolution, for \(m \geq 1\) as the kernel \(\ker(J^m \to J^{m+1})\)).
736	  The map \(J^m \to Z^{m+1}\) is the corestriction of the differential
737	  \(d^m : J^m \to J^{m+1}\) onto its image \(Z^{m+1} = \operatorname{im}(d^m)\), hence
738	  an epimorphism. Exactness in the middle is exactness of the resolution at \(J^m\):
739	  the image of \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) equals \(\ker(d^m)\) --- for \(m \geq 1\)
740	  by the definition \(Z^m = \ker(J^m \to J^{m+1})\), and for \(m = 0\) because
741	  exactness of \(0 \to A \to J^0 \to J^1\) makes \(A \hookrightarrow J^0\) identify
742	  \(A\) with \(\ker(d^0)\) --- while the kernel of the epimorphism
743	  \(J^m \twoheadrightarrow Z^{m+1}\) is exactly \(\ker(d^m)\). Thus
744	  \(\operatorname{im}(Z^m \to J^m) = \ker(J^m \to Z^{m+1})\), so \((S_m)\) is short
745	  exact. The middle term \(J^m\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic by hypothesis. The final
746	  identity \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\)
747	  is the case \(m = 0\): \(\operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0) = \operatorname{coker}(Z^0 \to J^0)
748	  \cong Z^1\) by \((S_0)\).
749	\end{proof}

779	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}; left-exactness yields an exact sequence

804	  \label{lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution}
805	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.cohomologyAppliedResolutionIso}
806	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}

811	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break

821	  \[

823	    \qquad

832	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
833	  The degree-zero statement is the \(n = 0\) case of
834	  Lemma~\ref{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles}: \(H^0(G(J^\bullet)) = \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1))
835	  \cong G(Z^0) = G(A)\). Fix now \(n \geq 1\). By definition of the cohomology of the
836	  cochain complex \(G(J^\bullet)\) in positive degree,
837	  \[
838	    H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr)
839	      \;=\; \ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr) \big/
840	            \operatorname{im}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\bigr).
841	  \]
842	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles} the numerator is
843	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) \cong G(Z^n)\), realized as the subobject
844	  \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\). Under this identification the differential
845	  \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\) lands inside \(G(Z^n)\): it factors as
846	  \(G(J^{n-1}) \xrightarrow{G(J^{n-1} \twoheadrightarrow Z^n)} G(Z^n)
847	  \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) by the factorization of \(J^{n-1} \to J^n\) through its image
848	  \(Z^n\), so its image equals the image of \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\) inside the
849	  subobject \(G(Z^n)\). Therefore the quotient
850	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) / \operatorname{im}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n))\) is exactly
851	  \(G(Z^n) / \operatorname{im}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)) =
852	  \operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n))\). Hence
853	  \(H^n(G(J^\bullet)) \cong \operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n))\).
854	\end{proof}

859	  \label{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived}

868	  % L5785--5811).

872	  % $A^\bullet$. The canonical map
873	  % $$
874	  % F(A^\bullet) \longrightarrow RF(A^\bullet)
875	  % $$

879	  % abelian categories. ... If every object of $\mathcal{A}$ injects into an object

881	  % and we obtain an exact functor

887	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015E (Leray's acyclicity
888	  lemma) and Tag 05TA (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}).}
889	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives, \(\mathcal{B}\)
890	  abelian, and \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) an additive left-exact functor.
891	  Let
892	  \[

894	      \longrightarrow J^0 \longrightarrow J^1 \longrightarrow J^2

896	  \]

9	# Acyclic resolutions compute right-derived functors
10	
11	This file provides the abstract homological-algebra core underlying the Čech
12	computation of higher direct images (Stacks Tag 015E, Leray's acyclicity lemma):
13	**an acyclic resolution computes every right-derived functor**.
14	
15	Throughout, `𝒜` and `ℬ` are abelian categories, `𝒜` has injective resolutions
16	(so that `G.rightDerived n` is everywhere defined), and `G : 𝒜 ⥤ ℬ` is an
17	additive functor.
18	
19	## Declarations
20	
21	* `CategoryTheory.Functor.IsRightAcyclic` — typeclass for right-`G`-acyclic objects.
22	* Instance: every injective object is right-acyclic (from Mathlib's
23	  `Functor.isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ`).

25	The following declarations are outlined in the strategy block below and will be
26	constructed by the prover in `mathlib-build` mode:
27

29	  lift `0 → A → B → C → 0` to a degreewise-split SES of injective resolutions.
30	* `CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfAcyclic` — dimension-shift
31	  isomorphism `(Rᵏ G)(Z) ≅ (Rᵏ⁺¹ G)(A)` across a SES with acyclic middle term.
32	* `CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedIsoOfAcyclicResolution` — main theorem:
33	  `(Rⁿ G)(A) ≅ Hⁿ(G(J•))` for any acyclic resolution `J•` of `A`.

63	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — middle-term quasi-isomorphism transfer
64	
65	Given a morphism `φ : S₁ ⟶ S₂` between two short exact sequences of homological complexes
66	in an abelian category, Mathlib proves that if `φ.τ₁` and `φ.τ₂` are quasi-isomorphisms then so
67	is `φ.τ₃` (`HomologicalComplex.HomologySequence.quasiIso_τ₃`). The companion statements for `φ.τ₁`
68	and `φ.τ₂` are an explicit Mathlib TODO (see `HomologySequenceLemmas.lean`). We supply the `φ.τ₂`
69	version here: it is the engine that proves the horseshoe middle complex `I_B` is an injective
70	*resolution* of `B` (the outer verticals `I_A.ι`, `I_C.ι` are quasi-isos, hence so is the middle
71	augmentation). The proof mirrors Mathlib's `τ₃` argument: a homology four-lemma on the windows of
72	`composableArrows₅`, with the boundary degrees (no predecessor / no successor) handled by
73	`mono_homologyMap_of_mono_of_not_rel` / `epi_homologyMap_of_epi_of_not_rel`. -/
74	
75	namespace HomologicalComplex.HomologySequence
76	
77	open CategoryTheory ComposableArrows Abelian Limits
78	
79	variable {C ι : Type*} [Category C] [Abelian C] {c : ComplexShape ι}
80	  {S₁ S₂ : ShortComplex (HomologicalComplex C c)} (φ : S₁ ⟶ S₂)
81	  (hS₁ : S₁.ShortExact) (hS₂ : S₂.ShortExact)
82	
83	include hS₁ hS₂ in
84	/-- **Middle-term quasi-isomorphism transfer** (the `τ₂` companion of Mathlib's `quasiIso_τ₃`).
85	If `φ.τ₁` and `φ.τ₃` are quasi-isomorphisms then so is `φ.τ₂`, provided that at each boundary
86	degree (one with no incoming / no outgoing differential) the middle component `φ.τ₂.f i` is a
87	mono / epi respectively. This is the `lean_aux` infrastructure behind
88	`InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact_resolvesMiddle`. -/
89	lemma quasiIso_τ₂ (h₁ : QuasiIso φ.τ₁) (h₃ : QuasiIso φ.τ₃)
90	    (hbMono : ∀ i, (∀ k, ¬ c.Rel k i) → Mono (φ.τ₂.f i))
91	    (hbEpi : ∀ i, (∀ j, ¬ c.Rel i j) → Epi (φ.τ₂.f i)) :
92	    QuasiIso φ.τ₂ := by
93	  have hI1 : ∀ d, IsIso (homologyMap φ.τ₁ d) := fun d => by
94	    rw [← quasiIsoAt_iff_isIso_homologyMap]; exact (quasiIso_iff φ.τ₁).1 h₁ d
95	  have hI3 : ∀ d, IsIso (homologyMap φ.τ₃ d) := fun d => by
96	    rw [← quasiIsoAt_iff_isIso_homologyMap]; exact (quasiIso_iff φ.τ₃).1 h₃ d
97	  have hE1 : ∀ d, Epi (homologyMap φ.τ₁ d) := fun d => have := hI1 d; inferInstance
98	  have hM1 : ∀ d, Mono (homologyMap φ.τ₁ d) := fun d => have := hI1 d; inferInstance
99	  have hE3 : ∀ d, Epi (homologyMap φ.τ₃ d) := fun d => have := hI3 d; inferInstance
100	  have hM3 : ∀ d, Mono (homologyMap φ.τ₃ d) := fun d => have := hI3 d; inferInstance
101	  rw [quasiIso_iff]
102	  intro i
103	  rw [quasiIsoAt_iff_isIso_homologyMap]
104	  have hEpi : Epi (homologyMap φ.τ₂ i) := by
105	    by_cases hi : ∃ j, c.Rel i j
106	    · obtain ⟨j, hij⟩ := hi
107	      apply epi_of_epi_of_epi_of_mono
108	        ((δlastFunctor ⋙ δlastFunctor).map (mapComposableArrows₅ φ hS₁ hS₂ i j hij))
109	      · exact (composableArrows₅_exact hS₁ i j hij).δlast.δlast
110	      · exact (composableArrows₅_exact hS₂ i j hij).δlast.δlast
111	      · exact hE1 i
112	      · exact hE3 i
113	      · exact hM1 j
114	    · have hi' : ∀ j, ¬ c.Rel i j := fun j hj => hi ⟨j, hj⟩
115	      have := hbEpi i hi'
116	      exact epi_homologyMap_of_epi_of_not_rel φ.τ₂ i hi'
117	  have hMono : Mono (homologyMap φ.τ₂ i) := by
118	    by_cases hi : ∃ k, c.Rel k i
119	    · obtain ⟨k, hki⟩ := hi
120	      apply mono_of_epi_of_mono_of_mono
121	        ((δ₀Functor ⋙ δ₀Functor).map (mapComposableArrows₅ φ hS₁ hS₂ k i hki))
122	      · exact (composableArrows₅_exact hS₁ k i hki).δ₀.δ₀
123	      · exact (composableArrows₅_exact hS₂ k i hki).δ₀.δ₀
124	      · exact hE3 k
125	      · exact hM1 i
126	      · exact hM3 i
127	    · have hi' : ∀ k, ¬ c.Rel k i := fun k hk => hi ⟨k, hk⟩
128	      have := hbMono i hi'
129	      exact mono_homologyMap_of_mono_of_not_rel φ.τ₂ i hi'
130	  exact isIso_of_mono_of_epi _
131	
132	end HomologicalComplex.HomologySequence

144	/-- An object `J : 𝒜` is *right-`G`-acyclic* when every higher right-derived
145	functor of `G` vanishes at `J`:
146	```
147	(Rᵏ⁺¹ G)(J) = 0   for all k : ℕ.
148	```
149	The index-shifted quantifier `k + 1` matches the statement of
150	`Functor.isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ` and avoids an inequality
151	side-condition; it is equivalent to `(Rⁿ G)(J) = 0` for all `n ≥ 1`.
152	
153	Blueprint: `CategoryTheory.Functor.IsRightAcyclic` (`def:right_acyclic`).
154	-/
155	class Functor.IsRightAcyclic (G : 𝒜 ⥤ ℬ) [G.Additive] (J : 𝒜) : Prop where
156	  vanish : ∀ k : ℕ, Limits.IsZero ((G.rightDerived (k + 1)).obj J)
157	
158	/-- Every injective object is right-`G`-acyclic.
159	Follows immediately from `Functor.isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ`. -/
160	instance (priority := 100) Functor.IsRightAcyclic.ofInjective
161	    (G : 𝒜 ⥤ ℬ) [G.Additive] (J : 𝒜) [Injective J] : Functor.IsRightAcyclic G J where
162	  vanish k := Functor.isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ G k J
163	-- Note: `Functor.isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ` returns
164	-- `Limits.IsZero ((G.rightDerived (k+1)).obj J)`, matching the class field.

184	  (Functor.IsRightAcyclic.vanish (G := G) (J := J) k).of_iso
185	    (I.isoRightDerivedObj G (k + 1)).symm

216	and with the middle object `J` right-`G`-acyclic, the connecting map of the homology long
217	exact sequence of `G(I_•)` is an isomorphism in every positive degree:
218	`(R^{k+1} G)(Z) ≅ (R^{k+2} G)(A)`.

221	It is stated over the *resolution-level* SES because the object-level dimension-shift theorem

224	noncomputable def Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfSplitResolutionSES
225	    (G : 𝒜 ⥤ ℬ) [G.Additive] {A J Z : 𝒜}
226	    (I_A : InjectiveResolution A) (I_J : InjectiveResolution J) (I_Z : InjectiveResolution Z)
227	    [G.IsRightAcyclic J]
228	    (φ : I_A.cocomplex ⟶ I_J.cocomplex) (ψ : I_J.cocomplex ⟶ I_Z.cocomplex)
229	    (w : φ ≫ ψ = 0)

231	      (HomologicalComplex.eval 𝒜 (ComplexShape.up ℕ) n)).Splitting)

244	/-- **Horseshoe per-stage monomorphism.** Given an exact short complex
245	`A → B → C` with `A → B` a monomorphism, and monomorphisms `α : A ↪ P`, `γ : C ↪ Q` with `P`
246	injective, the map `B → P ⊞ Q` whose first component is the injective extension of `α` along
247	`A ↪ B` (`Injective.factorThru α S.f`) and whose second component is `B ↠ C ↪ Q` is itself a
248	monomorphism. This is the cokernel/kernel step driving each stage of the dual Horseshoe Lemma
249	`InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact`: applied to the `n`-th cosyzygy short exact sequence with
250	`P = I_A.X (n+1)`, `Q = I_C.X (n+1)`, it produces the monomorphism into the next biproduct term
251	whose cokernel feeds the following stage. -/
252	lemma mono_biprod_lift_factorThru_of_exact {S : ShortComplex 𝒜} (hS : S.Exact) [Mono S.f]
253	    {P Q : 𝒜} [Injective P] (α : S.X₁ ⟶ P) [Mono α] (γ : S.X₃ ⟶ Q) [Mono γ] :
254	    Mono (biprod.lift (Injective.factorThru α S.f) (S.g ≫ γ)) := by
255	  rw [Preadditive.mono_iff_cancel_zero]
256	  intro T x hx
257	  have h1 : x ≫ Injective.factorThru α S.f = 0 := by simpa using hx =≫ biprod.fst
258	  have h2 : x ≫ S.g ≫ γ = 0 := by simpa using hx =≫ biprod.snd
259	  have hxg : x ≫ S.g = 0 := by rw [← cancel_mono γ, zero_comp, Category.assoc]; exact h2
260	  have hyf : hS.lift x hxg ≫ S.f = x := hS.lift_f x hxg
261	  have hya : hS.lift x hxg ≫ α = 0 := by
262	    have h3 : hS.lift x hxg ≫ S.f ≫ Injective.factorThru α S.f = 0 := by
263	      rw [← Category.assoc, hyf]; exact h1
264	    rwa [Injective.comp_factorThru] at h3
265	  have hy0 : hS.lift x hxg = 0 := by rw [← cancel_mono α, zero_comp]; exact hya
266	  rw [← hyf, hy0, zero_comp]

268	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — twisted biproduct of cochain complexes
269	
270	This block builds the *structural* core of the dual Horseshoe Lemma as a general, injective-free
271	construction. Given two cochain complexes `K`, `L` and a degreewise family
272	`τ n : L.X n ⟶ K.X (n+1)` satisfying the cocycle identity
273	`L.d n (n+1) ≫ τ (n+1) = -(τ n ≫ K.d (n+1) (n+2))`, the biproduct objects `K.X n ⊞ L.X n` with the
274	twisted matrix differential `[[d_K, τ], [0, d_L]]` form a cochain complex `twistedBiprod τ hτ`,
275	the coprojection `K ⟶ twistedBiprod` and projection `twistedBiprod ⟶ L` are chain maps, and every
276	degree is the canonical split short exact sequence of the biproduct.
277	
278	This is exactly the content of the blueprint sub-lemmas `lem:horseshoe_dComp` (the differential
279	squares to zero) and `lem:horseshoe_chainMap` (the coprojection/projection are chain maps and the
280	degrees split); it is isolated here free of injectivity because the only inputs are the cocycle
281	identity and the biproduct structure. The horseshoe specialises it to `K = I_A.cocomplex`,
282	`L = I_C.cocomplex` once the twist `τ` has been produced by injectivity. -/
283	
284	section TwistedBiprod
285	
286	omit [HasInjectiveResolutions 𝒜]
287	
288	variable {K L : CochainComplex 𝒜 ℕ} (τ : ∀ n, L.X n ⟶ K.X (n + 1))
289	
290	/-- The twisted matrix differential `[[d_K, τ], [0, d_L]]` on the degreewise biproduct
291	`K.X n ⊞ L.X n ⟶ K.X (n+1) ⊞ L.X (n+1)`. -/
292	noncomputable def twistedBiprodD (n : ℕ) :
293	    (K.X n ⊞ L.X n) ⟶ (K.X (n + 1) ⊞ L.X (n + 1)) :=
294	  biprod.lift (biprod.fst ≫ K.d n (n + 1) + biprod.snd ≫ τ n) (biprod.snd ≫ L.d n (n + 1))
295	
296	@[reassoc (attr := simp)]
297	lemma twistedBiprodD_fst (n : ℕ) :
298	    twistedBiprodD τ n ≫ biprod.fst = biprod.fst ≫ K.d n (n + 1) + biprod.snd ≫ τ n := by
299	  simp [twistedBiprodD]
300	
301	@[reassoc (attr := simp)]
302	lemma twistedBiprodD_snd (n : ℕ) :
303	    twistedBiprodD τ n ≫ biprod.snd = biprod.snd ≫ L.d n (n + 1) := by
304	  simp [twistedBiprodD]
305	
306	variable (hτ : ∀ n, L.d n (n + 1) ≫ τ (n + 1) = -(τ n ≫ K.d (n + 1) (n + 2)))
307	
308	include hτ in
309	lemma twistedBiprodD_comp (n : ℕ) :
310	    twistedBiprodD τ n ≫ twistedBiprodD τ (n + 1) = 0 := by
311	  apply biprod.hom_ext
312	  · simp only [Category.assoc, twistedBiprodD_fst, Preadditive.comp_add,
313	      twistedBiprodD_fst_assoc, twistedBiprodD_snd_assoc, zero_comp]
314	    rw [Preadditive.add_comp, Category.assoc, Category.assoc, HomologicalComplex.d_comp_d,
315	      comp_zero, zero_add, ← Preadditive.comp_add, hτ n, add_neg_cancel, comp_zero]
316	  · simp only [Category.assoc, twistedBiprodD_snd, twistedBiprodD_snd_assoc, zero_comp]
317	    rw [HomologicalComplex.d_comp_d, comp_zero]
318	
319	/-- The twisted biproduct cochain complex with differential `[[d_K, τ], [0, d_L]]`. -/
320	noncomputable def twistedBiprod : CochainComplex 𝒜 ℕ :=
321	  CochainComplex.of (fun n => K.X n ⊞ L.X n) (twistedBiprodD τ) (twistedBiprodD_comp τ hτ)
322	
323	@[simp]
324	lemma twistedBiprod_X (n : ℕ) : (twistedBiprod τ hτ).X n = (K.X n ⊞ L.X n) := rfl
325	
326	@[simp]
327	lemma twistedBiprod_d (n : ℕ) : (twistedBiprod τ hτ).d n (n + 1) = twistedBiprodD τ n :=
328	  CochainComplex.of_d _ _ _ _
329	
330	/-- The coprojection `K ⟶ twistedBiprod τ hτ`, degreewise `biprod.inl`. -/
331	noncomputable def twistedBiprodInl : K ⟶ twistedBiprod τ hτ where
332	  f n := biprod.inl
333	  comm' i j hij := by
334	    obtain rfl : i + 1 = j := hij
335	    simp only [twistedBiprod_d]
336	    apply biprod.hom_ext <;> simp
337	
338	/-- The projection `twistedBiprod τ hτ ⟶ L`, degreewise `biprod.snd`. -/
339	noncomputable def twistedBiprodSnd : twistedBiprod τ hτ ⟶ L where
340	  f n := biprod.snd
341	  comm' i j hij := by
342	    obtain rfl : i + 1 = j := hij
343	    simp [twistedBiprod_d]
344	
345	@[simp]
346	lemma twistedBiprodInl_f (n : ℕ) : (twistedBiprodInl τ hτ).f n = biprod.inl := rfl
347	
348	@[simp]
349	lemma twistedBiprodSnd_f (n : ℕ) : (twistedBiprodSnd τ hτ).f n = biprod.snd := rfl
350	
351	lemma twistedBiprodInl_comp_Snd : twistedBiprodInl τ hτ ≫ twistedBiprodSnd τ hτ = 0 := by
352	  ext n
353	  simp
354	
355	/-- Each degree of `0 → K → twistedBiprod → L → 0` is the canonical split short exact sequence of
356	the biproduct `K.X n ⊞ L.X n`. -/
357	noncomputable def twistedBiprodSplitting (n : ℕ) :
358	    ((ShortComplex.mk _ _ (twistedBiprodInl_comp_Snd τ hτ)).map
359	      (HomologicalComplex.eval 𝒜 (ComplexShape.up ℕ) n)).Splitting where
360	  r := biprod.fst
361	  s := biprod.inr
362	  id := by simpa using biprod.total
363	
364	end TwistedBiprod

366	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — the horseshoe twist family
367	
368	Given a short exact sequence `ses : 0 → A → B → C → 0` and chosen injective resolutions
369	`I_A`, `I_C`, this block constructs the off-diagonal twist family
370	`τ n : I_C.X n ⟶ I_A.X (n+1)` together with the augmentation first component, satisfying the
371	cocycle identity `d_C n ≫ τ (n+1) = -(τ n ≫ d_A (n+1))`. Each `τ` is produced by the universal
372	lifting property of injectives (`Injective.factorThru` / `ShortComplex.Exact.descToInjective`)
373	against the cosyzygy monomorphisms of `I_C`. This is the blueprint sub-lemma `lem:horseshoe_twist`
374	(the recursion kernel). Combined with the `twistedBiprod` construction above it yields the middle
375	complex of the dual Horseshoe Lemma. -/
376	
377	namespace InjectiveResolution
378	
379	section OfShortExact
380	
381	variable {ses : ShortComplex 𝒜} (hses : ses.ShortExact)
382	  (I_A : InjectiveResolution ses.X₁) (I_C : InjectiveResolution ses.X₃)
383	
384	/-- First component `B ⟶ I_A^0` of the horseshoe augmentation: the injective extension of the
385	augmentation `A ⟶ I_A^0` along the monomorphism `A ↪ B`. -/
386	noncomputable def horseshoeβ₁ : ses.X₂ ⟶ I_A.cocomplex.X 0 :=
387	  @Injective.factorThru _ _ _ _ _ (I_A.injective 0) (I_A.ι.f 0) ses.f hses.mono_f
388	
389	@[reassoc (attr := simp)]
390	lemma f_comp_horseshoeβ₁ : ses.f ≫ horseshoeβ₁ hses I_A = I_A.ι.f 0 :=
391	  @Injective.comp_factorThru _ _ _ _ _ (I_A.injective 0) (I_A.ι.f 0) ses.f hses.mono_f
392	
393	/-- Auxiliary map `C ⟶ I_A^1` through which `β₁ ≫ d_A^0` factors (since it kills `A`). -/
394	noncomputable def horseshoeH : ses.X₃ ⟶ I_A.cocomplex.X 1 :=
395	  hses.exact.descToInjective (horseshoeβ₁ hses I_A ≫ I_A.cocomplex.d 0 1) (by

426	/-- The recursion carrying, at each degree `n`, consecutive twists `τⁿ`, `τⁿ⁺¹` together with the
427	cocycle identity `d_C^n ≫ τⁿ⁺¹ = -(τⁿ ≫ d_A^{n+1})`. The step uses the lifting property of the
428	injective `I_A^{n+3}` against the cosyzygy mono of `I_C` (`exact_succ`); the base uses the
429	augmentation exactness `exact₀`. -/
430	noncomputable def twistPair : (n : ℕ) →
431	    Σ' (t0 : I_C.cocomplex.X n ⟶ I_A.cocomplex.X (n + 1))
432	       (_t1 : I_C.cocomplex.X (n + 1) ⟶ I_A.cocomplex.X (n + 2)),
433	       I_C.cocomplex.d n (n + 1) ≫ _t1 = -(t0 ≫ I_A.cocomplex.d (n + 1) (n + 2))
434	  | 0 => ⟨horseshoeτZero hses I_A I_C,
435	      I_C.exact₀.descToInjective (-(horseshoeτZero hses I_A I_C ≫ I_A.cocomplex.d 1 2))
436	        (horseshoeτZero_hf hses I_A I_C),
437	      I_C.exact₀.comp_descToInjective _ _⟩
438	  | (n + 1) =>
439	      let p := twistPair n
440	      ⟨p.2.1,
441	        (I_C.exact_succ n).descToInjective (-(p.2.1 ≫ I_A.cocomplex.d (n + 2) (n + 3))) (by
442	          have e : (-(p.1 ≫ I_A.cocomplex.d (n + 1) (n + 2))) ≫ I_A.cocomplex.d (n + 2) (n + 3)
443	              = 0 := by
444	            rw [Preadditive.neg_comp, Category.assoc, HomologicalComplex.d_comp_d, comp_zero,
445	              neg_zero]
446	          rw [Preadditive.comp_neg, neg_eq_zero, ← Category.assoc, p.2.2]
447	          exact e),
448	        (I_C.exact_succ n).comp_descToInjective _ _⟩

457	      -(horseshoeτ hses I_A I_C n ≫ I_A.cocomplex.d (n + 1) (n + 2)) :=
